paragraphs.body: The process of transitioning to Pittsburgh wasn’t easy. Adjusting to a new city, a different but familiar language, and living with new people was complicated. I had my whole family moving me into my new dorm. This act may seem normal to many, but it means a lot to me. My parents divorced in 2022, and ever since, the amount of time we spent together could be counted on one hand. For this reason, the fact that we were together as a family for this new chapter made me remember and realize certain things I learned throughout my time before college. They could sense I was anxious. I knew that they were going to leave. I had this feeling in my chest that felt like I had blocks of cement weighing me down. I could cry at any given moment from the knot I felt in my throat. Every minute that passed, I knew I wouldn’t have them as close as I would want them to be. I couldn’t feel protected by my mom's arms if anything went wrong. If I needed advice from my dad, I would need to call him, which doesn’t sound bad, but I wish I could have him right in front of me, reassuring me that everything is going to be okay and that everything I set my mind to, I would achieve it. The day they had to leave arrived, and that’s the first time I think I felt genuine sadness and pain. Surprisingly, I realized it wouldn’t be that bad after they left. I also realized that what made me sad was that after I came back from my activities or classes, they wouldn’t be here, and I feared I would get used to the fact that after the activities I did the first days of college, they would be there waiting for me. In the first few weeks, I didn’t miss home much. I was having fun with my friends and was distracted by the fact that I was thousands of miles away from my family. Later, it started to hit again. I started to call my sister more often. She also went to college in the United States and knows a lot about living away from home, so she greatly understands me. And the fact that she’s my sister makes me feel comfortable. My sister and I are six years apart. It was easy to notice the age gap when we were little because we didn’t get along very well. While she was in high school, I was in middle school, so we didn’t really have anything to bond over. It was hard for me because, as a middle child, I felt nobody had the same interests as me. My sister was sixteen and in high school, and my little brother was five and in kindergarten. It didn’t seem that long, so my sister would have to attend university. Now that I look back in time, I feel the time we spent together in our home went by in the blink of an eye. I didn’t have a phone when my sister went to college, so I didn’t talk to her as much. I remember vividly the first time she came home from her first semester in college. I returned from school, and she was waiting in the living room for my brother and me to return and surprise us. Tears fell off our faces, but those were tears of happiness. I instantly learned how much she felt like home. Now that we live in different states, she makes me feel at home even though I usually see her on my phone. When I visit her in Orlando, even though it is evidently not Puerto Rico, I feel at home because of her. She understands me and knows me more than anyone. If someone told me that my sister would feel at home, it would not make sense to me at all back then. But now it feels like the most truthful thing someone could say.

annotations.text: My parents divorced in 2022, and ever since, the amount of time we spent together could be counted on one hand.

paragraphs.body: My sister and I took this leap of faith in studying in the United States to have a bright future and make our parents proud. They have worked so hard all their lives for us to have what we have now. My parents’ priority was for my siblings and me to get the best education. After all, they have done for us, the least we could do is reciprocate the same effort to them by working hard and chasing these big dreams they have helped us get closer to. I couldn’t be where I am without their help. Specifically, their sayings that have been passed on from generation to generation. Puerto Rico has a lot of sayings that have a lot of meaning to them and can easily resonate with what is happening in your life. I consider my parents the wisest people I have met. This is due to how my grandparents raised them and what they taught them. They know what to say every time I have a problem. I don’t know if they always know what to say or if they make it up, but it always seems to make me feel better, and I always learn something new about the situation. When I was little, I had a lot of friendship problems. I cried some nights, wondering if I would ever have a healthy friendship. I sat down to talk with my parents because they would help me. They told me a saying I still remember and use today. "Después de la tormenta, viene la calma" / "After the storm, comes the calm”. And it couldn't be truer. Even though those times were tough, they taught me what I wanted in a friendship. As a result, I have a friend group in which I feel good and I can be myself. There hasn’t been an experience where I haven’t learned anything about it. I have learned that it doesn’t necessarily mean it will be something concrete, but growth as a person. It is frustrating to be at times when you feel everything is going against you and the world is falling on top of you. Trying to think everything will be okay when everything goes wrong is hard. I could say that I thought I wasn’t going to be able to overcome the fact that my sister wasn’t going to live in our home anymore, my parent’s divorce, moving into a new place far away from home, and that I wasn’t going to live in the house where once all five of us were together. I can proudly say that I overcame those tough times, although it took a long time. All of it shaped me into the strong, independent woman I am today, and I am grateful for that. The calm always comes, and most of the time, it is accompanied by many new things that make you grow as a person.

paragraphs.body: Moving away from home may seem as if you are never coming back. In fact, you create your meaning of home away from home. Home isn’t four walls, a floor, and a ceiling—that’s a house. Home is your family, your culture, and how it molds you to be this amazing human being. You get to discover yourself alongside so many new things. It is certainly daunting, but everything in life happens for a reason, and it is important to remember why you are where you are and how you got there.

annotations.text: Home isn’t four walls, a floor, and a ceiling—that’s a house. Home is your family, your culture, and how it molds you to be this amazing human being.

paragraphs.body: To live in a rural area in Puerto Rico means knowing What your family has done for you will carry on wherever you go, Or how far you go from the little island you were born in. The genuine friendships you make will stay in touch with you, Because even though it’s hard to believe, true friendships do exist. The wisdom Mom and Dad shared with you will accompany you the same way it has accompanied our family tree
